final_answer: To locate the bottleneck, first collect gateway error logs with timestamps (N1:0:194aea7d, N3:0:0c81ec28) and correlate them with backend latency spikes (N3:2:1b2e042b). Enable distributed tracing (N1:1:2b6d6315, N3:1:4be134e8) to measure latency of each downstream HTTP call and database query. Compare post‑release metrics with baseline (N1:2:36c3bcfd) to see changes in request rate, DB CPU, connection‑pool usage, and thread counts. Profile the database: enable the slow‑query log (N2:0:137d8edf), run EXPLAIN ANALYZE on high‑CPU queries (N2:1:5cf867db), inspect lock contention (N2:2:bc8ef45e), and verify the application connection‑pool size (N2:3:00debf2e). Identify any slow or failing downstream services and add appropriate timeouts and circuit‑breaker settings (N1:3:b91f3a96, N3:7:45d33d16) to prevent hangs. Verify backend thread/worker pool sizes and queue lengths (N1:5:20ef186b) and increase them if saturation is observed. Check gateway and load‑balancer timeout settings (N3:5:fc80b594) and raise them if backend processing exceeds defaults. If errors disappear after a temporary rollback or disabling new features (N1:6:19bfa72f), the release is likely the cause. To stabilize the system, implement rate limiting or back‑pressure at the gateway (N1:7:942dc067), scale the database vertically or add read replicas (N3:8:d0898ba6), and add caching for frequent read queries (N3:9:89342830).
